subject: Erbium
executive_summary:
Erbium is a heavy rare earth element whose strategic importance derives primarily from its irreplaceable role in erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs), the technology that underpins virtually all long-distance fiber optic communications, including secure military communication networks, submarine cables, and data center interconnects. The EDFA market is valued at approximately $898 million in 2025, capturing 63% of the global optical amplifier market, and is projected to reach $1.7 billion by 2034. Every fiber optic signal traveling more than 100 kilometers relies on erbium-doped amplifiers to boost the optical signal without converting it to electrical form.

China controls the processing of erbium alongside other rare earths, with approximately 85-90% of global processing capacity. In October 2025, China expanded its export licensing requirements to specifically include erbium, along with holmium, thulium, europium, and ytterbium, in addition to the seven elements controlled since April 2025. This brought the total to 12 of 17 rare earth elements under Chinese export restrictions. While these controls were suspended in November 2025 as part of the U.S.-China trade agreement through November 2026, the military end-user ban remains in force.

The U.S. has no independent commercial-scale erbium separation capacity. Erbium is extracted as a byproduct of broader rare earth processing from xenotime and monazite ores. Emerging recycling efforts are recovering erbium from decommissioned telecom hardware and old fiber optic cables, but these urban mining operations remain small relative to demand. The defense and intelligence communities' complete dependence on erbium-doped fiber for secure communications makes this supply chain a critical national security concern.

key_findings.headline: Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers are the backbone of all military and intelligence fiber optic communications
key_findings.detail: Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs) are the only proven technology for amplifying optical signals in the 1550 nm wavelength window used by virtually all long-distance fiber optic networks. Military secure communications, intelligence community networks, submarine cable systems, and defense data center interconnects all depend on EDFAs. There is no commercially viable substitute that provides equivalent performance across the C-band and L-band wavelengths critical to high-capacity fiber optic transmission.

key_findings.headline: China expanded export controls to include erbium in October 2025
key_findings.detail: China's Ministry of Commerce added erbium to its export licensing requirements in October 2025 as part of an expansion covering five additional rare earth elements (erbium, holmium, thulium, europium, ytterbium). Companies must apply for export licenses with 45-day review periods, and the controls apply to erbium oxide, alloys, compounds, and products containing more than 0.1% by value of controlled materials. The military end-user ban effective December 1, 2025 applies to all controlled rare earths including erbium.

key_findings.headline: EDFA market growing at 7.16% CAGR, driven by 5G, data centers, and defense networks
key_findings.detail: The global EDFA market is valued at $898 million in 2025, capturing 63% of the optical amplifier market, and is projected to reach $1.7 billion by 2034 at a 7.16% CAGR. Growth is driven by 5G network buildouts requiring dense fiber infrastructure, hyperscale data center interconnects, and military communications modernization. This expanding demand intensifies pressure on an already concentrated erbium supply chain.

key_findings.headline: U.S. has zero domestic commercial-scale erbium separation capacity
key_findings.detail: The United States does not currently have a reliable, end-to-end domestic supply chain for processed erbium. Erbium is extracted as a byproduct of broader rare earth processing from xenotime and monazite minerals. While MP Materials and Energy Fuels are developing rare earth separation capabilities, erbium-specific separation circuits are not among the near-term priorities, which focus on higher-value neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium, and terbium.

key_findings.headline: Erbium recycling from telecom waste emerging but remains small-scale
key_findings.detail: Labs and companies in technology hubs are beginning to recover usable erbium from decommissioned telecom hardware and old glass fibers. Mining companies are also investing in tailings reprocessing, with automation in rare earth extraction improving recovery yields from erbium-bearing minerals. However, these urban mining and recycling operations remain far too small to serve as a meaningful alternative to primary production from Chinese-processed ores.

key_findings.headline: Section 232 investigation finds processed rare earth imports threaten national security
key_findings.detail: The January 2026 Section 232 proclamation specifically identified the lack of domestic processing capacity for rare earth elements including erbium as a national security threat. The investigation found that despite being the world's second-largest producer of unprocessed rare earth minerals, the U.S. lacks extensive refining capacity, making it reliant on foreign producers for derivative products essential to defense and telecommunications infrastructure.

recent_developments.headline: China expands rare earth export controls to include erbium
recent_developments.summary: China's Ministry of Commerce expanded export licensing requirements to cover erbium, holmium, thulium, europium, and ytterbium, effective November 8, 2025. This brought the total number of rare earth elements under Chinese export controls to 12 of 17. The controls require per-shipment licensing with 45-day review periods and apply to products containing more than 0.1% by value of controlled materials, including fiber optic amplifier components.
recent_developments.impact: Directly threatened the global fiber optic infrastructure supply chain. Telecommunications equipment manufacturers and defense contractors faced potential disruption to erbium-doped fiber amplifier production and maintenance of existing fiber networks.

recent_developments.headline: China suspends erbium export controls through November 2026
recent_developments.summary: As part of the U.S.-China trade agreement, China suspended implementation of export controls on all recently controlled rare earth elements including erbium for one year through November 2026. However, the military end-user ban effective December 1, 2025 remains in force, meaning defense-related fiber optic applications cannot source erbium through Chinese channels.
recent_developments.impact: Provided temporary relief for commercial telecom applications but created a two-tier market: commercial users can access Chinese erbium under licensing, while defense and intelligence applications face effective embargo conditions.

recent_developments.headline: Section 232 proclamation identifies processed critical minerals as national security threat
recent_developments.summary: President Trump issued a Section 232 proclamation finding that imports of processed critical minerals including rare earths threaten national security. The proclamation directed negotiators to pursue supply agreements with allied nations within 180 days, with potential tariffs or price floor mechanisms under consideration for rare earth products.
recent_developments.impact: Created policy framework for addressing erbium supply chain vulnerability through trade agreements and potential protective measures. The 180-day deadline of July 2026 for negotiation progress signals urgency but not immediate action.

recent_developments.headline: U.S.-Australia framework agreement includes rare earth processing expansion
recent_developments.summary: The $8.5 billion U.S.-Australia critical minerals framework agreement includes provisions for expanding rare earth processing capacity at Australian and allied facilities, potentially creating alternative sources for heavy rare earths including erbium. The Export-Import Bank issued over $2.2 billion in financing letters for critical mineral projects.
recent_developments.impact: Establishes financing pathways for diversifying erbium supply away from Chinese sources, though new processing facilities will take years to reach commercial production.

risk_assessment.current_risk: Erbium presents a high supply chain risk with uniquely strategic implications for communications infrastructure. While erbium lacks the headline visibility of neodymium or dysprosium in permanent magnets, its irreplaceable role in fiber optic amplification makes it a single point of failure for the entire telecommunications backbone -- military and civilian alike. China's 85-90% processing dominance, combined with the October 2025 export controls (suspended but not withdrawn), demonstrates Beijing's ability to disrupt global fiber optic supply chains at will. The U.S. has no domestic erbium separation capacity and no near-term plans to develop one, as investment priorities focus on magnet-related rare earths. The military end-user ban creates immediate risk for defense and intelligence communication networks.
key_drivers: China controls 85-90% of erbium processing with explicit export licensing now in place
Erbium is irreplaceable in EDFAs that underpin all long-distance fiber optic communications
Military end-user export ban effective December 2025 creates defense embargo conditions
No U.S. domestic erbium separation capacity exists or is planned near-term
Growing EDFA demand from 5G, data centers, and defense modernization increases supply pressure
Temporary export control suspension expires November 2026 with no renewal certainty
mitigation_actions: Include erbium separation in planned MP Materials or Energy Fuels heavy rare earth processing circuits
Build strategic stockpile of erbium oxide and erbium-doped fiber preforms for defense communications
Accelerate erbium recycling from decommissioned telecom equipment and fiber cables
Fund research into alternative optical amplification technologies that reduce erbium dependency
Negotiate erbium-specific supply agreements with Australia and other allied rare earth producers
Include erbium-doped fiber in defense supply chain reshoring mandates alongside permanent magnets
Support Lynas and other non-Chinese processors to add erbium separation capability
